item.title: TB cases soar in Morobe
item.description: MOROBE is ranked second in tuberculosis (TB) cases for four consecutive years, with cases exceeding 6,000 annually since 2022, according to the Morobe Provincial Health Authority’s annual review. The National Capital District (NCD) is ranked above Morobe.
item.content:encoded:
MOROBE is ranked second in tuberculosis (TB) cases for four consecutive years, with cases exceeding 6,000 annually since 2022, according to the Morobe Provincial Health Authority’s annual review.

The National Capital District (NCD) is ranked above Morobe.

East Sepik, Eastern Highlands and Madang are third, fourth and fifth respectively.

“For TB, Morobe is number two in the country,” TB programme officer Akisa Kawe told the review.

Of the 6,366 cases recorded last year, Lae district accounted for 4,464, nearly seven in every ten provincial diagnoses.

She said the figures placed Lae in a category of its own, with the next highest district, Huon Gulf, recording 413 cases.

Bulolo followed with 331 cases, Nawaeb with 317, Tawae-Siassi with 278, Markham with 264, Finschhafen with 171, Menyamya with 120, and, Kabwum with eight cases.

Within Lae, the disease is concentrated in specific communities.

Haikost recorded the highest burden at 824 cases, followed by the Centre of Mercy with 764, Tent City with 613, Butibam with 575, Buimo with 504, Malahang with 420, and Taraka with 360.

All are areas defined by dense housing, poor ventilation, and limited access to early diagnosis and treatment.

Kawe said the trend was not isolated to Morobe.

A national comparison of all provinces shows last year’s TB figures are the highest on record in nearly every province, with the NCD and Morobe towering above the rest.

Kawe said the sustained high burden in Lae’s informal settlements pointed to a failure of detection, and not a failure of treatment.

“If we have a lot of TB cases in children, that means there are lots of infectious TB cases or sources out in the public that are still not being detected.”

Her warning was backed by four years of paediatric data.

Last year, 1,063 of Morobe’s TB patients were children, representing 24 per cent of all cases, a proportion that has held steady since 2022.

Children acquire TB through sustained contact with infectious adults.

She noted that a persistently high paediatric rate signals that a large reservoir of undiagnosed, infectious cases remains in the community.

Kawe called for intensified active case finding in high-burden communities as an immediate priority.

“The work of finding and treating those undetected cases remains one of our most urgent priorities.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: More graduates needed, says Rosso
item.description: DEPUTY Prime Minister and Lae MP John Rosso has stressed the need to increase the number of graduates in medicine and health science to meet the growing demand on health care in the country. “The Angau Memorial (Hospital in Lae) requires 900 professionals to serve – and that is just one province,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
DEPUTY Prime Minister and Lae MP John Rosso has stressed the need to increase the number of graduates in medicine and health science to meet the growing demand on health care in the country.

“The Angau Memorial (Hospital in Lae) requires 900 professionals to serve – and that is just one province,” he said.

“As our population grows (to more than) 11 million this year, there will be a need for more professionals in the health sector and in health education.”

He said that while more than 400 medical students graduated from the University of PNG last week, the country still needed thousands more to fill the gaps in the heath sector.

In October last year, Parliament passed a bill to establish a PNG university to allow students to specialise in various health fields.

“It reflects a long standing vision to elevate medical and health sciences training into a specialised institution capable of producing doctors, nurses, researchers and technical professional required to meet our national needs,” he said.

Last year, the medical faculty graduated 334 in 37 programmes.

In 2026, more than 400 health professionals graduated, with 60 in the post-graduate level, 24 newly trained medical specialists in surgery, internal medicine, obstetrics and gynecology.

The undergraduate level produced more than 300 graduates, with 100 new medical doctors, alongside pharmacists, laboratory scientist, radiographics and nurses in areas such as intensive care, corrective care, mid-wife duties, and mental health. These are front line workers.

“We also acknowledge diploma workers who will serve in essential frontline roles taking the weight of cities and towns and remote communities where health services are most needed.

“These figures are not just numbers, they represent lives that will be saved, communities that will be served and the nation that will be strengthened,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: School of Medicine’s final graduation
item.description: UNIVERSITY of Papua New Guinea’s (UPNG) School of Medicine and Health Sciences (SMHS) has produced more than 1,000 health professionals since 2022. Friday marked the school’s final graduation affiliated with UPNG.
item.content:encoded:
UNIVERSITY of Papua New Guinea’s (UPNG) School of Medicine and Health Sciences (SMHS) has produced more than 1,000 health professionals since 2022.

Friday marked the school’s final graduation affiliated with UPNG.

It begins a new journey as the PNG University of Medicine and Health Sciences in January next year.

Chancellor Sir Robert Igara said this was about transition, legacy and continuity.

“Our first ceremony about 56 years ago in 1970, a total of 14 graduates received awards and out of these, seven were Papuans and New Guineans,” he said.

“Over these past five years since 2022, the SMHS has produced a total of 1,507 graduates; this includes today’s (Friday) 318 graduates which includes international graduands mainly from Pacific island families.

He said women made up 52 per cent of the graduates.

Sir Robert said the story of the school was deeply woven into the history of UPNG since becoming part of the university in 1957, standing a pillar of excellence in health education.

“Over the decades it has grown into the school of medicine and health sciences. Producing generation of doctors, nurses, public health professionals and researchers who have served PNG with distinction,” he said.

“Its impact is not only measured in qualifications awarded but in life saved, community strengthened and futures transformed from the most remote villages to our largest hospitals.

“Your journey has not been easy, you have endured long hours of study, demanding clinical training and long hours of uncertainty, yet you persisted and in doing so you have demonstrated qualities that define excellence in the health professions discipline, resilience and compassion.”

“You will face challenges like limit resources, demanding environments and difficult decisions and with that full knowledge you remain steadfast in your commitment in your chosen field throughout the next phase of your journey.”

“As we look ahead to the new establishment the foundation laid at UPNG since 1967 will continue to guide and strengthen the new institution.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Marape challenges government agencies, MPs to work with urgency
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape has urged Cabinet ministers and department heads to deliver tangible results, with less than four parliamentary sittings left before the General Election 2027 With global economic conditions and natural disasters gradually taking their toll, he stressed the need for a renewed commitment to progress the nation.
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape has urged Cabinet ministers and department heads to deliver tangible results, with less than four parliamentary sittings left before the General Election 2027

With global economic conditions and natural disasters gradually taking their toll, he stressed the need for a renewed commitment to progress the nation.

“These challenges are not new to us. We had faced Covid-19, major natural disasters, and political turbulence in the past,”he said. 

“We will get through this as we always have, with resilience and determination.”

He reminded ministers and department heads that it “is now May”.

“I expect all ministers and department heads to be fully at work, progressing their responsibilities with urgency and discipline,” he said.

Drawing from his recent visit to China, Marape reflected on the work ethic and discipline observed in one of the world’s largest economies.

“I shared with ministers and Members of Parliament that in China, public servants and leaders are not paid higher than those in Papua New Guinea. That was surprising,” he said.

“Yet the level of commitment, the discipline, and the sheer volume of work they put into building their country speaks volumes.

“From a developing nation in the 1970s and 1980s, they have risen to become a global economic powerhouse, driven by research, innovation, and hard work.”

Marape instructed all Government agencies to submit progress reports outlining achievements and implementation status.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Falcons hand Hunters’ sixth loss
item.description: THE SP PNG Hunters now record six losses of this year’s Hostplus Cup season after going down 28-8 to the Sunshine Coast Falcons in Round 8 at the Santos National Football Stadium on Saturday. The defeat leaves the Hunters with just three wins so far this season, adding pressure as the competition moves deeper into the year.
item.content:encoded:
THE SP PNG Hunters now record six losses of this year’s Hostplus Cup season after going down 28-8 to the Sunshine Coast Falcons in Round 8 at the Santos National Football Stadium on Saturday.

The defeat leaves the Hunters with just three wins so far this season, adding pressure as the competition moves deeper into the year.

Despite the win, Falcons head coach Brad Henderson admitted the match came at a heavy physical cost.

“Yeah, look, it’s good to get through it and good to get a win, but I think there’s going to be some damage there,” Henderson said.

He said the tough nature of the game was no surprise, especially playing in Port Moresby.

“Coming over here to PNG, you know you’re going to cop it physically,” he said. “You wouldn’t meet a more passionate group of players than the PNG boys.”

The coach believes his side is improving week by week. However, he admitted errors are still an issue.

“I feel like the last two weeks we’ve been showing improvement. We’re starting to control the ball a lot more, particularly in key moments,” he said. “Sometimes we turn over the ball early, but a lot of that came from the Hunters’ physicality.”

Henderson said this win felt more deserved compared to previous close results.

“It’s just nice to get the two points,” he said. “Last year we did that and I don’t know if we deserved it, but today I felt like we did.”

Injury-wise, young forward Billy Lawrence is facing a worrying knee injury. Zoran Johnson also picked up a shoulder injury, with Henderson describing it as “pretty decent”.

“We think it might be meniscus. He’s done his ACL before so, hopefully, he’s okay,” he added.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Orchids get new coach
item.description: THE Papua New Guinea Orchids (national women’s rugby league team) has a new head coach, and he is Craig Sandercock (pictured). Sandercock is the current head coach of the Wests Tigers women’s team in Australia’s National Rugby League (NRL) competition.
item.content:encoded:
THE Papua New Guinea Orchids (national women’s rugby league team) has a new head coach, and he is Craig Sandercock (pictured).

Sandercock is the current head coach of the Wests Tigers women’s team in Australia’s National Rugby League (NRL) competition.

The PNG Rugby Football League (PNGRFL) said in a statement on Friday that Sandercock brings a wealth of elite-level experience to the Orchids programme as they prepare for the upcoming Rugby League World Cup.

“Sandercock has an impressive coaching pedigree, including previous roles with the Manly Sea Eagles, Newcastle Knights, Hull KR (Super League), and various international programmes.

“He was instrumental in the development of Canterbury Bulldogs women’s programme prior to their entry into the NRLW competition in 2025. His appointment signals a major step in the ongoing professionalisation and development of the national women’s team,” the PNGRFL said.

PNGRFL chairman Sandis Tsaka said: “We are delighted to welcome Craig Sandercock to lead the Orchids.

“Craig’s experience in the NRLW competition, coupled with his background in the Super League, makes him the ideal candidate to take the Orchids to the next level. His commitment to developing players and building a strong team culture aligns perfectly with our vision for the future of women’s rugby league in PNG.”

In response, Sandercock said: “I am genuinely excited by the opportunity to coach the PNG Orchids.

“Having lived in Papua New Guinea for two years as a child, this role feels like a full-circle moment for me. I am impressed by the potential of the local players — their natural talent, athleticism, and passion for rugby league are outstanding.

“I look forward to working with all the players to harness that potential, build a competitive squad, and prepare strongly for the World Cup.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pora Oval buzzes as Wewak kicks off preseason
item.description: PORA Oval in Wewak, East Sepik, came alive on Saturday as the local league launched its preseason competition across all divisions. Among the senior and junior teams of the Wewak Rugby Football League (WRFL) were the Meni Sharks senior men’s and Under-20 sides. Club president Pascal Esi explained that the Sharks draw players from Siau and surrounding communities near Meni village, uniting youths a
item.content:encoded:
PORA Oval in Wewak, East Sepik, came alive on Saturday as the local league launched its preseason competition across all divisions.

Among the senior and junior teams of the Wewak Rugby Football League (WRFL) were the Meni Sharks senior men’s and Under-20 sides. Club president Pascal Esi explained that the Sharks draw players from Siau and surrounding communities near Meni village, uniting youths and seniors in a healthy, purposeful activity.

The Meni Sharks senior men showed determination, holding Zulu Raiders to a 4-4 draw. Other senior matches saw Wewak Tigers edge Henimba Jets 4-0, while Maprik United and Wariman Bulls finished scoreless. In the U17 division, Bishop Leo Secondary and Brandi Secondary also shared a 4-4 draw.

Sharks manager Malcolm Limbai praised his team’s effort but highlighted areas for improvement, particularly ball-handling and reducing missed tackles.

He emphasised refining these aspects in upcoming training sessions. Supporting the club’s operations are treasurer Judith Kumasi, head coach Ludwick Arey, and trainer Manuel Anis.

In a milestone for East Sepik, an Under-18 Girls rugby league trial was staged at Pora Oval. Maprik Secondary and Passam National High School battled to a 0-0 draw after 30 minutes of play.

League executives confirmed that the women’s competition will officially begin next month.

Meanwhile, Agmark East Sepik School League trials also unfolded over the weekend.

In the U17 division, Yangoru Secondary faced Maprik Secondary, followed by U19 clashes between Maprik Secondary and Wosera Secondary, and St Xaviers against Passam National High School. Bishop Leo also met Brandi Secondary in the U19 Boys division.

East Sepik Rugby League development director Bradley Simon highlighted the importance of these trial matches, thanking the community and local businesses for their ongoing support.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PETE’S PUNCHLINE
item.description: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
item.content:encoded: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Conserve land for sanctuaries
item.description: A RECENT front-page article highlighted the endangered Bird of Paradise, a symbol of our national pride. Its survival is crucial, and the state institutions responsible for protecting our unique flora and fauna must act urgently to prevent extinction caused by mining, forestry, and agricultural activities.
item.content:encoded:
A RECENT front-page article highlighted the endangered Bird of Paradise, a symbol of our national pride.

Its survival is crucial, and the state institutions responsible for protecting our unique flora and fauna must act urgently to prevent extinction caused by mining, forestry, and agricultural activities.

The departments of Environment and Climate Change, Arts, Culture and Tourism, and Agriculture must collaborate to address the rapid depletion of species like the Bird of Paradise, mountain eagle, cassowary, and possum.

A solution lies in establishing sanctuaries and natural habitats through environmental conservation efforts.

Areas licensed by the Department of Environment and Climate Change and Forestry could be converted into protected sanctuaries for wildlife.

Conservation methods should complement each other, promoting ecotourism while preserving these species.

Officers trained in environmental and natural sciences should utilise their expertise by creating nurseries and artificial nests to safeguard endangered plants and animals, ensuring their survival for future generations.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Action on youth unemployment crisis
item.description: I AM writing to express my concern regarding the rising number of Grade 10, Grade 12, and university school-leavers across Papua New Guinea who remain unemployed. The Government previously pledged to create 2,000 jobs for school-leavers, but this promise has not been fulfilled. As a result, many young people are left idle in villages, streets, towns, and cities, worsening unemployment and poverty.
item.content:encoded:
I AM writing to express my concern regarding the rising number of Grade 10, Grade 12, and university school-leavers across Papua New Guinea who remain unemployed.

The Government previously pledged to create 2,000 jobs for school-leavers, but this promise has not been fulfilled. As a result, many young people are left idle in villages, streets, towns, and cities, worsening unemployment and poverty.

Without opportunities, many young people are being drawn into unlawful activities, such as theft, violence, and crimes related to sorcery accusations.

The legal process for those caught in criminal activities is slow and often lacks resolution.

While some school-leavers have access to land and resources to sustain themselves, many others are left without support, contributing to social problems.

In today’s world, survival and development rely on financial means, and every child deserves guidance and support.

The Government, as the caretaker of the nation, has a responsibility to address these concerns.

It is alarming to see so many young people neglected and without direction.

 I urge the Government to take immediate, practical action to create meaningful opportunities for school-leavers and ensure a brighter future for our youths.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Roadmap to create jobs
item.description: THE growing challenge of graduate unemployment in Papua New Guinea is undeniable. Graduation is a moment of pride, but for many, it quickly gives way to uncertainty as job opportunities remain scarce and competition intensifies.
item.content:encoded:
THE growing challenge of graduate unemployment in Papua New Guinea is undeniable.

Graduation is a moment of pride, but for many, it quickly gives way to uncertainty as job opportunities remain scarce and competition intensifies.

This creates emotional and financial pressure on graduates and their families.

Each year, more graduates enter the workforce, but the formal job market isn’t expanding fast enough to accommodate them.

Additionally, there is often a mismatch between the skills graduates acquire and the needs of employers.

A practical national response should focus on clear pathways.

First, a national graduate transition programme could offer internships, apprenticeships, and short-term placements to help graduates gain valuable experience.

Second, investment in district-based economic sectors like agriculture and small-scale processing would create more local job opportunities.

Third, an small-to-medium enterprises support system could provide funding, mentorship, and business training to encourage small enterprise creation.

Education institutions must also align more closely with industry needs, emphasising practical skills and entrepreneurship.

The private sector should receive incentives to hire and train graduates.

Finally, a national service pathway could engage graduates in community development roles, building skills and discipline.

With coordinated action and practical reforms, Papua New Guinea can transform this challenge into an opportunity for inclusive growth.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Two-award recipient Margaret declares:With God, nothing is impossible
item.description: MARGARET Stanley dedicated the two awards she received at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy’s graduation on Thursday to her parents for their sacrifice, and to her siblings who expect her to lead by example. “I carry the weight of what my parents went through. So I have come this far because I know that my parents are looking up to me. And my siblings are also
item.content:encoded:
MARGARET Stanley dedicated the two awards she received at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy’s graduation on Thursday to her parents for their sacrifice, and to her siblings who expect her to lead by example.

“I carry the weight of what my parents went through. So I have come this far because I know that my parents are looking up to me. And my siblings are also looking up to me, plus my family members too. I made it to university, and I need to lead by example.”

Margaret, 23, received the Dean’s Award and the Human Resource Management top-student award. She graduated with a Bachelor of Business Management degree.

She is from Bwagaoia, Alhoga and Gulewa villages of Samari-Murua district in Milne Bay. She is the eldest of three children.

“My dad is a senior pastor at the CRC (Christian Revival Crusade) church, and my mother is one of the staff there, doing church work as well.”

She regards herself fortunate to have such a strong Christian upbringing.

“One thing that I’m always proud of is that God sees me through all of these challenges. It was all because of Him and through the help of the Holy Spirit.”

She appreciates God’s blessings plus her parents’ mentoring duties.

“It was through His help, plus my parents always imparting their wisdom and their knowledge. Although they are limited in that space, I still enjoy their guidance and listen to them.”

Margaret chose the course because she wants to serve the people just like her parents.

“When I was in high school, I chose Social Science as I wanted to study international relations. However, Covid-19 hit and I had to change my course. What course can I take that would align with what I want and would not take me away from my parents?

“I looked at my strength and what I love doing. My parents meet people, get to know people, understand their situations and preach.

“In the work space, that’s human resource management. That’s why I chose human resource management.”

Her biggest challenge at university was peer pressure.

“I thank the Lord and my parents for their corrections and guidance. I would not have set my boundaries and know when to go and when not to go.”

“One thing that I’m always proud of is that God sees me through all of these challenges. It was all because of Him and through the help of the Holy Spirit.”

“My mum gives me advice and wisdom. My dad also gives me advice and corrects me as well, and sets boundaries.”

She is currently attached with a financial institution through its graduate development programme.

Her advice to young people is to remain focused.

“You can have fun but stay focused. Know where your priorities are and always put God first.”

She was “nerve-cited” when her name was called during the graduation.

“I was nervous standing up where everybody’s watching me. I was excited as well because that’s the goal that I’ve been working towards.”

She knew the sacrifices and commitments had paid off when she received the awards.

“In all honesty, and not to sound boastful, it was the years of dedication, sacrifices and also understanding what matters most. Especially in university with so many distractions and peer pressure, you have to understand how to balance that out.

“And I had confidence in myself that I’ll receive the award. I didn’t want to sound too overconfident, but understanding how far I’ve come and what I’ve done and the effort that I put in, I was definitely going to get that award.”

“When I look at it, or when my siblings look at it, they know that Margaret didn’t do it alone. She did it with the help of God.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rachael dedicates 3 awards to parents and family
item.description: RACHAEL Onopia dedicated the three awards she received yesterday at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy graduation in Port Moresby to her eldest sister Samantha and late father. “My Dad was a police officer. He died when I was in Grade Nine. That really pushed me to focus on my studies. And my Mum, she’s not working but supports me in whatever way she can.”
item.content:encoded:
RACHAEL Onopia dedicated the three awards she received yesterday at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy graduation in Port Moresby to her eldest sister Samantha and late father.

“My Dad was a police officer. He died when I was in Grade Nine. That really pushed me to focus on my studies. And my Mum, she’s not working but supports me in whatever way she can.”

Rachael, 22, is from Kasuo village in Chuave, Chimbu. She is the youngest of three sisters.

She graduated with a Bachelor of Accounting degree. She received an award from the Certified Practicing Accountants (CPA), the Vice-Chancellor’s award, and for being the top student in the Accounting division.

She graduated with a Diploma in Accounting in 2023.

She had wanted to study law but during her foundation year found that accounting was more appealing. It also had a little to do with finance.

“Honestly, accounting wasn’t my first choice. It was law. But because of financial constraint, I had to choose a business course.

“And while doing my business management foundation year, accounting was really challenging. It captivated me. So I want something challenging.”

She attributed her success to good time management.

“Key takeaways are consistency and time management. When we are consistent in something, the habit builds up over time. And when we manage our time properly to invest in things that will benefit us, we reap the benefits. And when we put in the hard work, God blesses us.”

Rachael was elated when she found out that she was going to graduate. 

“I mean, it was really exciting when I saw my name on the list.”

She was not expecting any special awards as well.

“But like I said, hard work pays off, plus God’s blessing.”

Her biggest challenge at the university was trying to pass two of the “hardest courses” – advance financial accounting and reporting one and two.

Rachael views education as the key to a better future.

“I’d say I had no one to turn to. With that idea in mind, my education was my only way out. So I invested everything I could in it.”

Rachael describes her eldest sister Samantha as her role model.

“She’s always been very supportive of me. She does not question whatever decision I make. She just supports me. And she goes out of her way to support me financially – and her husband as well.”

I’d say I have no one to turn to. So with that idea in mind, my education is my only way out. So I invested everything I could in it.”

Her strength lies in getting things done under tight deadlines, getting her priorities right, and never giving up.

“Nothing is impossible. You do your best and sometimes the benefits will exceed your expectations. Be disciplined and consistent. They pay off.”

She is now attached with an accounting firm through its graduate development programme.

Eldest sister Samantha, who flew in from West New Britain to attend the graduation ceremony yesterday, is proud of her younger sibling.

“It was hard. Every time when it was time to pay tuition fees, it was some kind of pressure. But God came through for us, every time. Even at the last minute, God is always there providing for our needs. God put people in our lives to see us through. So all thanks to God and my husband’s family. I just wish Dad was here.”

“Firstly, I dedicate my degree to my dad because he always believes in me. When I was still in elementary, he would say: ‘You are going to university. You are going to graduate.’ And here I am.

“Secondly, I want to dedicate all these achievements to my eldest sister and her husband who supported me financially throughout my university life. This is for them and my family who prayed for me constantly.

 “To say that I’m proud is an understatement.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific needs disaster displacement included in treaty
item.description: When disasters strike, so does displacement. Each year across the Pacific, cyclones, flooding, coastal erosion and storm surges prompt people to leave their homes.
item.content:encoded:
When disasters strike, so does displacement.

Each year across the Pacific, cyclones, flooding, coastal erosion and storm surges prompt people to leave their homes.

Often this movement is temporary, as people seek shelter in evacuation centres or on higher ground, returning home after the immediate danger has passed.

But sometimes it becomes prolonged or even permanent.

For many Pacific communities, displacement is not an exceptional outcome of disasters; it is a recurring and defining feature of how they are experienced.

Although Pacific countries are on the frontline of the climate crisis, they are also at the forefront of solutions.

The Pacific Regional Framework on Climate Mobility, endorsed by Pacific Leaders in 2023, is a world-leading document that seeks to ensure that the region is resilient and “future-ready” by anticipating, preparing for and responding to the adverse impacts of climate change and disasters, including through mobility.

The region has a new opportunity to show global leadership in this area.

Between now and early 2027, states are negotiating what is likely to become the first-ever global treaty on the Protection of Persons in the Event of Disasters (PPED treaty).

This presents a significant opening for Pacific states to ensure that displacement is reflected in the treaty text.

Despite how central displacement is to the lived reality of disasters, it remains only marginally reflected in the current draft treaty text.

The proposed treaty aims to fill a longstanding gap in international law by establishing a comprehensive legal framework to strengthen the protection of people affected by disasters, including through greater international cooperation and disaster risk reduction (DRR).

The negotiation process is now entering a critical stage. States were invited to submit written proposals for amendments to the draft text by the end of last year.

These proposals were discussed during a session of a dedicated United Nations (UN) Working Group early this month.

These discussions will inform a consolidated draft expected by September 2026, with a view to adoption at a diplomatic conference scheduled for early next year in the Philippines.

And yet, within this process, one issue stands out for its relative absence: disaster-related displacement.

This omission of displacement is striking given its scale and significance globally.

Disasters displace two persons every second.

In 2024 alone, an estimated 65.8 million internal displacements were recorded, with almost 70 per cent triggered by disasters rather than conflict.

Although the current definition of “disaster” (in draft article 3) mentions “mass displacement” as a possible defining characteristic of a disaster, it is otherwise missing from the treaty – and some proposals would remove even this reference.

The silence on displacement in the PPED treaty is not merely a technical oversight.

It risks sidelining one of the most common and significant consequences of disasters at precisely the moment when states are crafting the legal framework intended to address them.

If left unaddressed, the treaty may fall short of capturing how disasters are actually experienced by millions of people worldwide.

For the Pacific, this gap is particularly consequential.

Disaster-related displacement is a defining feature of the region’s security.

Pacific Island countries are among the most exposed in the world to cyclones, flooding, droughts and sea-level rise.

In some cases, entire communities face the prospect of displacement, which is why a number of Pacific states have developed national policies addressing internal displacement, as well as planned relocation guidelines and operating procedures.

Meanwhile, the Falepili Union between Tuvalu and Australia creates a special human mobility pathway which enables up to 280 Tuvaluans annually to migrate to Australia.

This leadership would be welcome in the PPED negotiations.

Pacific states have not yet made any formal proposals on the treaty text, and representation from the region in the Working Group discussions has also been limited.

item.title: Editha on track for art and design career
item.description: EDITHA Tubal has always loved arts and design since she was young, and yesterday, she graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in that very field – arts and design. “Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”
item.content:encoded:
EDITHA Tubal has always loved arts and design since she was young, and yesterday, she graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in that very field – arts and design.

“Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”

She scored a GPA of 4.33 to top her class at the University of PNG School of Humanities and Social Sciences. She described that achievement as “humbling”, after years of sleepless nights and struggling to achieve her goals.

Editha, 32, is the second last-born in a family of five. Her parents, Akuila Tabal and Fidelma Tubal, are from the villages of Taranga in Gazelle district, and Karavi in Kokopo, East New Britain.

Her mother Fidelma is a retired businesswoman. Her late father Akuila was a former public servant in East New Britain.

“I would like to dedicate (my degree) to both my parents, my mum and my late dad.”

She credits her mother as being the driving force behind her success after her dad passed away. She ensured that Editha was well and focused on her studies.

“My biggest supporter was my mum. She called me every day to check on how things were. That enabled me to come up with good grades.”

Her family including her mother, who flew in from East New Britain to support her on her big day, were at the Sir John Guise stadium yesterday to witness her graduation and to congratulate her.

Editha never doubted the path she was going to take regarding her career as she knew that art was what she wanted to pursue.

Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”

From her early passion about art and design, Editha knew that her strength lay in her imaginative and creative traits. They helped her in “deciding things and putting them down on paper”.

Her biggest challenge at university were the nights when she would stay up late to finish her projects.

“The biggest challenge (for me) was probably the sleepless nights during the last few projects.”

Never giving up and always showing up in class helped her achieve her goal.

“I just made sure that I showed up every day. That’s half the work done. I showed up every day and I tried to do the best I could.”

Her support system are her friends, family and, of course, God.

Her word of advice to those still in school pursuing the same programme in the same field of art and design as her is to keep at it.

“It is very enjoyable. They can learn a lot from it when they are taking it.”

A career in art and design often focuses on the blend of creativity, practice, and the necessity of persistence.

Pablo Picasso, one of the most influential artists of the 20th century, when asked about art and design, wrote: “Inspiration exists, but it must find you working,”

And for Editha, earning the top award in her class means the world to her.

“It means a lot to me. I am very happy and humbled. And it makes me want to pursue a career in this field more.”
